description: Tech founders debating startup history and economics. Contains confident false claims about company valuations and timelines.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Instagram had only 13 employees when Facebook acquired them for a billion dollars in 2012.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: Thirteen employees and about 30 million users at the time. It was insane. Best acquisition ever.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: WhatsApp was even crazier. Facebook bought them for 19 billion in 2014. They had like 55 employees and 450 million users.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: YouTube was bought by Google for 1.65 billion in 2006. And it was only about 18 months old at that point.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Right, Chad Hurley, Steve Chen, and Jawed Karim founded it. First video was uploaded in April 2005, that 'Me at the zoo' clip.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: Twitter was founded in 2006 by Jack Dorsey, Biz Stone, Evan Williams, and Noah Glass. The first tweet was by Dorsey in March 2006.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: And then Elon bought it for 44 billion in 2022 and renamed it to X. Probably the worst acquisition in tech history.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: I'd argue Yahoo buying Tumblr for 1.1 billion in 2013 was worse. They sold it to Automattic for reportedly under 3 million in 2019.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Amazon started in Jeff Bezos's garage in Seattle in 1994. He chose Seattle because of the talent pool from Microsoft.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: Actually I think there were multiple reasons, including Washington state having no income tax. But yeah, he started selling books online.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Stripe is worth more than SpaceX now, right? Like 95 billion or something?

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: No way. SpaceX was valued at around 180 billion as of late 2024. Stripe's valuation dropped to like 50 billion from its peak of 95 billion.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Moore's Law says computing power doubles every 18 months.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: It's actually about transistor count on chips doubling every two years, not 18 months. Gordon Moore's original 1965 paper predicted every year, then he revised it to every two years in 1975.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: The dot-com bubble burst in March 2000. The NASDAQ peaked at over 5,000 points.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: 5,048 on March 10, 2000, to be exact. Took until 2015 to get back to that level.

transcript.speaker: Kai
transcript.text: Bitcoin was created by Satoshi Nakamoto in 2008. The whitepaper came out October 31, 2008, and the genesis block was mined January 3, 2009.

transcript.speaker: Nora
transcript.text: The first real-world Bitcoin transaction was those two pizzas for 10,000 BTC in May 2010. Bitcoin Pizza Day. Those pizzas would be worth billions now.

expected_claims.text: Moore's Law says computing power doubles every 18 months
expected_claims.speaker: Kai
expected_claims.correction: Moore's Law refers to transistor count doubling approximately every two years, not computing power every 18 months. The 18-month version conflates Moore's Law with related observations.
